title: Conjecture 1.1 (Le's Induced Paths Conjecture)
statement_text: There exists $c > 0$ such that every $2\mathcal{O}$-free graph $G$ has at most $|G|^c$ induced paths.
context_text: Ngoc Khang Le proposed this conjecture (unpublished, [4]) as the stronger of two conjectures motivated by the search for a polynomial-time algorithm to recognise $2\mathcal{O}$-free graphs. A graph is $s\mathcal{O}$-free if no $s$ of its cycles are pairwise vertex-disjoint and anticomplete. If the conjecture holds, one can enumerate all induced paths, check each pair of induced cycles formed by extending them, and decide $2\mathcal{O}$-freeness in polynomial time.
paper_contribution: Proves the conjecture in full, and its generalisation to all $s\geq 1$: for every integer $s\geq 1$ there exists $c>0$ such that every $s\mathcal{O}$-free graph $G$ has at most $|G|^c$ induced paths (Theorem 1.2), yielding a poly-time algorithm to test $s\mathcal{O}$-freeness for any fixed $s$.
notes: PDF source; the symbol rendered as 'O' throughout the text is a cycle/circle symbol, here approximated as $\mathcal{O}$. The conjecture is formally labelled '1.1 Conjecture' in the paper with attribution to Le in the surrounding prose.
paper_title: Induced paths in graphs without anticomplete cycles

title: Le's Induced Cycles Conjecture
statement_text: There exists $c > 0$ such that every $2\mathcal{O}$-free graph $G$ has at most $|G|^c$ induced cycles.
context_text: This weaker conjecture of Le is mentioned alongside the stronger path conjecture (Conjecture 1.1) in the introduction; both were proposed in the unpublished manuscript [4] in an attempt to find a polynomial-time recognition algorithm for $2\mathcal{O}$-free graphs.
paper_contribution: Proved as an immediate consequence of Theorem 1.2, which establishes the stronger statement for induced paths and for all $s\geq 1$.
notes: Stated only in prose in the introduction, not in a labelled theorem environment. PDF source; 'O' approximated as $\mathcal{O}$.
paper_title: Induced paths in graphs without anticomplete cycles
